request.question: What university located in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States was Omer Dean Ehlers the first athletic director for?

title: Ted Toogood
Leo Harris
W. A. Lambeth
Ernest Becker (athletic director)
James Madison University
Dean Ehlers
Wilbur Wade Card
Harrisonburg High School (Virginia)
Mel Lucas
Tennessee Volunteers
sentences: Alexander Edgar (Ted) Toogood (August 27, 1924 – July 31, 2011) was a Canadian football player who played for the Toronto Argonauts.
He won the Grey Cup with them in 1950 and 1952.
He previously played football for and attended the University of Toronto, as well as served in World War II.
He later was a teacher, attended West Virginia University (MSc 1958) and was the first athletic director at Ryerson University (1949–1961).
Toogood was inducted into the University of Toronto Sports Hall of Fame in 1999, the Ryerson Athletics and Recreation Hall of Fame in 2005 and the Etobicoke Sports Hall of Fame in 2010.
He died on July 31, 2011 aged 86 and donated his brain to the Krembil Neuroscience Centre Sports Concussion Project.

At the time of his death, he had Alzheimer's disease and studies of his brain revealed the presence of chronic traumatic encephalopathy, though to be linked to previous concussions sustained in his football career.
Leo A. Harris (August 6, 1904 – April 22, 1990) was an American athlete, coach, and athletic director.

He played college football at Stanford University, coached football and basketball at Fresno State College, and was the first athletic director for the University of Oregon, bringing success to a financially troubled system.
He was also known for his handshake deal with Walt Disney that permitted the University of Oregon to use the likeness of Donald Duck as the basis for its mascot, the Oregon Duck.
William Alexander Lambeth (October 27, 1867 – June 24, 1944) was a medical professor who was the first athletic director at the University of Virginia.
He is often called "the father of intercollegiate athletics" at the university.
Ernest Becker (1914 – January 5, 1998) was an American tennis player, coach, and college athletics administrator.
He was a founding dean, first athletic director, and important influence at the beginning of Orange County State College, which became California State University, Fullerton.
According to the Orange County Register, Becker "typed the first college catalog and launched the first student newspaper.
He also staged an amazingly successful attention-getting event designed to put the young campus on the map: the 'First Intercollegiate Elephant Race in Human History,' held in 1962.
It started as a joke but drew more than a dozen entries from universities around the world and was covered in 82 news publications.
The elephants were rented from a Hollywood studio, and the largest was ridden by the editor of the Harvard University student newspaper, the Crimson."
Becker started out at Orange County State College as the founding Dean of Students in 1959 when he first came to the school.
He was an Emeritus Philosophy Professor.
Becker was a major factor in choosing the school's athletic team nickname, the Titans.
James Madison University (also known as JMU, Madison, or James Madison) is a public coeducational research university located in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States.

Founded in 1908 as the State Normal and Industrial School for Women at Harrisonburg, the institution was renamed Madison College in 1938 in honor of President James Madison and then James Madison University in 1977.
The university is situated in the Shenandoah Valley, with the campus quadrangle located on South Main Street.
Omer Dean Ehlers (June 22, 1929 – February 19, 2017) was an American college basketball and baseball coach and athletic administrator.
He was the first athletic director for James Madison University and served for 22 years.
Wilbur Wade "Cap" Card (October 29, 1873 – September 3, 1948) was an American baseball player, coach and athletic director at Duke University.

He initially introduced college basketball to the state of North Carolina and became the university's first men's coach of that program from 1906 to 1912 as well as being Duke's first athletic director from 1902 to 1948.
Harrisonburg High School (HHS), part of the Harrisonburg City School System, is a public high school located in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States.
HHS serves grades nine through twelve, and its athletic teams are known as the Blue Streaks.
There are currently 1,604 students enrolled.
It was rated "Fully Accredited" by the Virginia Department of Education for the 2006–2007 school year.
Mel Lucas is a former college baseball coach and college administrator.
He served as head coach of the Troy Trojans baseball team before being hired as the first athletic director at South Alabama, then a new university in Mobile, Alabama.
He served as the school's first baseball coach for four seasons before hiring former major leaguer Eddie Stanky in order to focus on his growing administrative duties.
Lucas was instrumental in forming the Sun Belt Conference and was a central figure in the growth of the Jaguars athletic programs.
He was inducted into the school's hall of fame in 1989.
The Tennessee Volunteers and Lady Volunteers are the 18 male and female varsity intercollegiate athletics programs that represent the University of Tennessee in Knoxville, Tennessee.
The Volunteers compete in Division I of the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) as a member of the Southeastern Conference (SEC).
In September 2011 Dave Hart, formerly the assistant athletic director at the University of Alabama, was introduced as Tennessee's new athletic director.

Hart became the school's first athletic director in Tennessee history to oversee the women's and men's athletic departments as they merged in June 2012 after which Joan Cronan, the former women's athletics director, became the senior adviser to Hart and Chancellor Jimmy Cheek.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What university located in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States was Omer Dean Ehlers the first athletic director for?
hop1_docs: Dean Ehlers | Omer Dean Ehlers (June 22, 1929 – February 19, 2017) was an American college basketball and baseball coach and athletic administrator.  He was the first athletic director for James Madison University and served for 22 years.

Harrisonburg High School (Virginia) | Harrisonburg High School (HHS), part of the Harrisonburg City School System, is a public high school located in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States.  HHS serves grades nine through twelve, and its athletic teams are known as the Blue Streaks.  There are currently 1,604 students enrolled.  It was rated "Fully Accredited" by the Virginia Department of Education for the 2006–2007 school year.

Eastern Mennonite Seminary | Eastern Mennonite Seminary (EMS) is the graduate theological division of Eastern Mennonite University (EMU), which is an accredited Christian liberal arts university in Harrisonburg, Virginia.  EMU was established as Eastern Mennonite College in 1917.  In 1965, after several decades of growth and change on the Harrisonburg campus, the seminary was re-established with its formal name and an acting dean.  EMS was an outgrowth of the curriculum in Bible at what was then known as Eastern Mennonite College.  EMU and EMS together are considered the embodiment of a vision held by the Mennonite leaders from Virginia, Maryland and Pennsylvania who first established EMC in 1917.

James Madison University | James Madison University (also known as JMU, Madison, or James Madison) is a public coeducational research university located in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States.  Founded in 1908 as the State Normal and Industrial School for Women at Harrisonburg, the institution was renamed Madison College in 1938 in honor of President James Madison and then James Madison University in 1977.  The university is situated in the Shenandoah Valley, with the campus quadrangle located on South Main Street.

James Madison Dukes women's basketball | The James Madison Dukes women's basketball team is the basketball team that represents James Madison University in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States.  The school's team currently competes in the Colonial Athletic Association.

Long Field at Mauck Stadium | Long Field at Mauck Stadium is a baseball venue located on the campus of James Madison University in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States.  It was home to the James Madison Dukes baseball team, a member of the Division I Colonial Athletic Association until the end of the 2009 season, when it was replaced by Eagle Field at Veterans Memorial Park.  Built in 1974, The venue has a capacity of 1,200.

Linville, Virginia | Linville is an unincorporated community located in Rockingham County, in the U.S. state of Virginia.  It is located 6 miles north of Harrisonburg, Virginia.  It contains the Linville United Church of Christ.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- "Omer Dean Ehlers (June 22, 1929 – February 19, 2017)" — Passage [1].
- "first athletic director for James Madison University and served for 22 years." — Passage [1].
- "James Madison University ... is a public coeducational research university located in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States." — Passage [4].

Ambiguities:
- None; "Omer Dean Ehlers" uniquely appears in Passage [1], and "James Madison University" is unambiguous in Passages [1] and [4].

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing bridge facts; passages explicitly link Ehlers to James Madison University and confirm that university's location in Harrisonburg, Virginia.

Retrieval direction:
- No further retrieval required. Final answer: James Madison University.
response.hop2_query: Omer Dean Ehlers James Madison University
hop2_docs: Dean Ehlers | Omer Dean Ehlers (June 22, 1929 – February 19, 2017) was an American college basketball and baseball coach and athletic administrator.  He was the first athletic director for James Madison University and served for 22 years.

James Madison Dukes softball | The James Madison Dukes softball team represents James Madison University in NCAA Division I college softball.  The team participates in the Colonial Athletic Association (CAA) and plays home games in Veterans Memorial Park.  JMU has won four CAA championships, including back-to-back Championships in 2016 and 2017, and has been to the NCAA Division I Softball Tournament six times, hosting Regionals and Super Regionals in 2016.  The team is coached by Mickey Dean.

James Madison University | James Madison University (also known as JMU, Madison, or James Madison) is a public coeducational research university located in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States.  Founded in 1908 as the State Normal and Industrial School for Women at Harrisonburg, the institution was renamed Madison College in 1938 in honor of President James Madison and then James Madison University in 1977.  The university is situated in the Shenandoah Valley, with the campus quadrangle located on South Main Street.
Omer Madison Kem | Omer Madison Kem (November 13, 1855 – February 13, 1942) was a Nebraska Populist politician.

Martha Boaz | Martha Boaz (1911-1995) was an American librarian.  She earned a B.S. in Library Science in 1937 from George Peabody College.  She was the assistant librarian at Carrier Library, then known as Madison Memorial Library, from 1940 to 1949.  The library was at James Madison University, then known as Madison College.  She received her Master's in Library Science in 1950 and her PhD in Library Science in 1955, both from the University of Michigan.  She was the first woman and the third person to earn a PhD in library science from the University of Michigan.  She was the dean of the University of Southern California School of Library Science from 1955-1978, its longest serving dean as of 2005, and some of her records are held at the University Archives of the University of Southern California.  She also served as President of the California Library Association from 1962-1963, Chair of its Research Committee from 1958-1960, Chair of its Intellectual Freedom Committee from 1964-1966, President of its Library Education Division from 1968-1969, on its Board of Directors in 1972, and as its Chair of the Council of Deans from 1977-1978.

James C. Hickman | James C. Hickman (August 27, 1927 – September 10, 2006) was an American actuary.  He was internationally publicized for his work in actuarial education as well as being a major contribution in the development of the actuarial profession.  He was a professor emeritus of business and statistics and former dean of the University of Wisconsin–Madison School of Business.

Mike Deane | Mike Deane (born September 27, 1951) is an American college basketball coach who most recently was a men's assistant coach at James Madison University.  He retired at the end of the 2017 basketball season.  He was previously head basketball coach at Wagner College and was relieved of his duties on March 1, 2010, after which he took a two-year hiatus from the game.  He had also held the head coach position at Siena College, Marquette University, and Lamar University.
response.summary_2: Omer Dean Ehlers was the first athletic director for James Madison University, a public research university located in Harrisonburg, Virginia, United States.
